rubric.B: Defends the hash map with examples but doesn't identify a case where an alternative is better
rubric.C: Either dismisses the claim without examples or agrees with it without nuance

M03.title: Assessment: Linear Traversal Patterns — Arrays, Pointers, Windows

questions.question: You are given a sorted array. You want to determine if any two elements sum to a target value T. Which approach is most space-efficient?

options.A: Use a hash set to store all elements, then check if T - nums[i] exists for each i
options.B: Use converging two pointers: lo=0, hi=len-1, advancing inward based on the sum
options.C: Use nested loops to check all pairs with early exit
options.D: Sort the array, then binary search for the complement of each element

questions.explanation: The array is already sorted. Converging two pointers gives O(n) time and O(1) extra space — no hash map needed. Option A is O(n) space. The array being sorted is the key precondition that makes two pointers applicable.

questions.question: Floyd's cycle detection (fast/slow pointers) detects a cycle in a linked list. What happens when fast and slow first meet?

options.A: Slow is at the head and fast is at the tail — the distance between them gives the cycle length
options.B: Both pointers are at the cycle's entry point
options.C: Both pointers are somewhere inside the cycle, not necessarily at the entry
options.D: Fast is exactly twice as far from the head as slow

questions.explanation: When fast and slow first meet, they are guaranteed to be inside the cycle, but the meeting point is not the cycle entry. To find the entry: reset one pointer to head and advance both by one step at a time — they will meet at the entry.

questions.question: Problem: minimum window substring containing all characters of T. During the shrink phase of the variable sliding window, when do you stop shrinking?

options.A: When the window length equals len(T)
options.B: When the window no longer contains all characters of T
options.C: When left pointer equals right pointer
options.D: When removing the leftmost character would not change the window's validity

questions.explanation: The shrink phase continues as long as the window is still valid (contains all characters of T), recording the minimum window length each iteration. You stop shrinking only when the window loses a required character and becomes invalid.

questions.question: Given `nums = [1, 1, 1]` and the prefix sum array `prefix = [0, 1, 2, 3]`, what is `sum(nums[1..2])` (0-indexed inclusive)?

options.D: prefix[3] - prefix[1] = 2

questions.explanation: Sum of nums[1..2] = prefix[3] - prefix[1] = 3 - 1 = 2. Prefix sum formula: sum(l, r) = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]. Options A and C are wrong values; D shows the formula and evaluates to 2, so B and D both represent the same correct answer — B is the numeric value.

questions.question: Problem: count subarrays with sum == k. You use prefix sums and a hash map initialised as `{0: 1}`. Why must the map be initialised with `{0: 1}` and not `{}`?

options.A: To handle the empty subarray edge case where sum equals k immediately from index 0
options.B: To prevent a KeyError when looking up prefix[0]
options.C: To ensure every prefix sum maps to at least 1 occurrence for counting
options.D: Initialising with 0 has no effect; it is a style convention only

questions.explanation: If prefix[i] == k, then the subarray nums[0..i] has sum k. The lookup checks map.get(prefix[i] - k, 0) = map.get(0, 0). Without {0: 1}, this lookup returns 0 and we miss the valid subarray starting from index 0.

questions.question: You are given an unsorted list of intervals. You want to find all pairs with the same sum. Sorting first reduces time complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n). This is valid only when:

options.A: The array has no duplicates
options.B: The sorted order enables a linear-time scan (e.g. two pointers) that wouldn't work on unsorted data
options.C: The array length is at least 100 elements
options.D: All elements are non-negative

questions.explanation: Sorting as preprocessing is only beneficial when sorted order unlocks a linear sub-algorithm (two pointers, binary search lookups). The total cost is O(n log n) sort + O(n) scan. If no linear sub-algorithm is possible on sorted data, sorting doesn't help.

questions.question: Which two-pointer sub-pattern is best suited for detecting a cycle in a linked list, and which is best for finding whether two values in a sorted array sum to a target?

options.A: Fast/slow for cycles; converging for pair sum
options.B: Converging for cycles; fast/slow for pair sum
options.C: Both problems use converging two pointers
options.D: Both problems require a hash map instead of pointers

questions.explanation: Fast/slow (Floyd's): both pointers traverse same direction at different speeds — designed for cycle detection and finding midpoints in linear structures. Converging: pointers start at opposite ends of sorted array — designed for pair search and three-sum problems.

questions.question: A problem asks for the maximum sum subarray of exactly length k. Write the algorithm in pseudocode using a fixed sliding window and state its time and space complexity.

required_points: Compute initial window sum for nums[0..k-1]
max_sum = window_sum
Slide: for i from k to len(nums)-1: window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i-k]; max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum)
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May use left/right pointer style instead of index offset; any equivalent formulation
answer_key.full_credit: Correct initial window, correct slide formula, correct complexities
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct logic but complexities wrong or slide formula incorrect

questions.question: Explain the key precondition that determines whether to use two pointers vs. a hash map for a pair-sum problem. Give one example where two pointers works and one where a hash map is necessary.

required_points: Precondition for two pointers: array must be sorted (or sortable without breaking problem constraints)
Two-pointer example: Two Sum on a sorted array — sorted enables converging pointers
Hash map example: Two Sum on an unsorted array where indices must be preserved — sorting would break index requirements
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any correct pair-sum example where sorting is blocked by index constraints
answer_key.full_credit: Identifies sortability as the deciding factor, gives one example for each
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly identifies the precondition without providing both examples

questions.prompt: The prefix sum and sliding window patterns both solve subarray problems in O(n) time. Compare them: what properties of a problem indicate prefix sum is the right tool, and what properties indicate sliding window? Give a concrete problem for each and explain how to recognise which to apply.
questions.guidance: A strong response distinguishes: prefix sum works on arbitrary range queries and subarray-sum-equals-k style problems; sliding window works on contiguous subarray optimisation with a shrinkable constraint. The distinction is whether the window is query-based (prefix) or constraint-driven (sliding window).

rubric.A: Clearly distinguishes when each applies with correct terminology, gives a concrete problem for each, and articulates the recognition signal
rubric.B: Correctly describes both techniques and gives examples but conflates when each is better
rubric.C: Describes one technique accurately, the other vaguely or incorrectly

M04.title: Assessment: Tree and Graph Traversal — BFS/DFS Duality

questions.question: You need to find the shortest path from node S to node T in an unweighted, undirected graph. Which traversal guarantees the shortest path found first is truly the shortest?

options.A: DFS with memoisation
options.B: BFS — it explores all nodes at distance d before any at distance d+1
options.C: Both BFS and DFS find the shortest path; BFS is just typically faster
options.D: Neither — you need Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest path

questions.explanation: BFS explores in layers: all nodes at distance 1, then 2, etc. The first time it reaches T, the path used is guaranteed to be via the minimum number of edges. DFS does not have this property — it may find a longer path first. Dijkstra's is needed for weighted graphs.

questions.question: In-order traversal of a Binary Search Tree produces nodes in what order?

options.A: Root, left subtree, right subtree
options.B: Left subtree, root, right subtree — ascending sorted order
options.C: Left subtree, right subtree, root
options.D: Level by level from root to leaves

questions.explanation: BST property: all left descendants < node < all right descendants. In-order (left, root, right) processes nodes in ascending order. This is the basis for BST-based sorting and finding the kth smallest element.

questions.question: DFS three-colour marking uses white, gray, and black. You are processing a directed graph DFS and you encounter an edge from the current node U to a gray node V. What does this tell you?

options.A: V has already been fully processed and the edge is a cross edge
options.B: V is currently on the DFS call stack — this edge closes a cycle (back edge)
options.C: V is an unvisited node and will be explored next
options.D: V and U are in different connected components

questions.explanation: Gray = on the current DFS call stack (currently being processed). An edge from U to gray V means V is an ancestor of U in the DFS tree — this creates a directed cycle. This is the cycle-detection mechanism in directed graph DFS.

questions.question: Kahn's topological sort processes a graph with n=5 nodes and produces a result list of length 4 before the queue is empty. What can you conclude?

options.A: One node was isolated (no edges) and was correctly excluded
options.B: The graph contains at least one directed cycle
options.C: The topological sort is valid but incomplete — restart from a different node
options.D: The graph has multiple valid topological orderings

questions.explanation: Kahn's adds a node to result only when its in-degree reaches 0. If len(result) < n, at least one node's in-degree never reached 0, which means it was part of a cycle where no node in the cycle could go first.

questions.question: Multi-source BFS starts with multiple source nodes in the queue simultaneously. What problem does this elegantly solve that single-source BFS cannot?

options.A: Shortest path in a weighted graph
options.B: Minimum distance from any one of several source nodes to every other node simultaneously
options.C: Topological ordering of a DAG
options.D: Cycle detection in a directed graph

questions.explanation: Multi-source BFS computes shortest distance from the nearest source in one pass. Example: '01 Matrix' finds the shortest distance to the nearest 0 for every cell. Single-source BFS would require running BFS from each source separately, giving O(S × (V+E)).

questions.question: You want to compute the maximum depth of a binary tree. Which traversal pattern gives the cleanest recursive implementation?

options.A: Pre-order — process the node before computing children depths
options.B: In-order — natural for BST problems but works for depth too
options.C: Post-order — compute left depth and right depth first, then return max + 1
options.D: Level-order BFS — count the number of levels

questions.explanation: Post-order naturally computes two child results then derives the parent result: `return 1 + max(maxDepth(left), maxDepth(right))`. Both left and right results are needed before the parent can return. BFS level-order also works but requires more code.

questions.question: DFS post-order topological sort represents the topological order as the reverse of the DFS finish times. Why is the reverse needed?

options.A: Because DFS processing order is right-to-left and must be corrected
options.B: A node finishes (turns black) after all its descendants finish. Later finish time = earlier in topological order, so reversing gives the correct dependency-first order
options.C: DFS produces a BFS-equivalent ordering that must be reversed to get depth-first order
options.D: Reversing ensures we satisfy Kahn's in-degree constraint

questions.explanation: In topological order, if A depends on B, B must come first. DFS finishes B (and all its descendants) before finishing A — so B has a later finish time. Reversing means later-finish = earlier position, giving B before A.

questions.question: Explain why a `visited` set is necessary for BFS on a general graph (not a tree), and describe what happens if you omit it.

required_points: Graphs can have cycles (unlike trees); without a visited set, BFS will revisit nodes
Revisiting leads to infinite loops — the queue never empties because already-visited nodes keep being re-enqueued
Space can also blow up as nodes are enqueued multiple times
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May describe the problem in terms of TLE or infinite loop
answer_key.full_credit: Identifies cycles as root cause, describes the infinite-loop consequence
answer_key.partial_credit: Identifies that nodes are revisited without explaining the cycle cause or consequence

questions.question: You must implement topological sort on a directed graph. Write the decision criteria for choosing Kahn's BFS vs. DFS post-order for each of these two requirements: (a) smallest lexicographic order, (b) detect a cycle and abort processing.

required_points: (a) Lexicographic order: Use Kahn's with a min-heap (priority queue) instead of a regular queue — dequeue the smallest available node first
(b) Cycle detection: Both work, but Kahn's is simpler — just check if len(result) < n. DFS requires maintaining gray/black state explicitly.
Neither approach is strictly always better; choose based on secondary requirement
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May mention that DFS post-order with a set for in-progress nodes is equivalent to three-color
answer_key.full_credit: Correctly addresses both requirements with specific implementation detail
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly addresses one requirement

questions.prompt: Compare BFS and DFS as complementary tools rather than competitors. Describe the specific problem properties that make each the correct choice, and explain at least one scenario where using the wrong traversal produces an incorrect — not just slow — result.
questions.guidance: A strong answer identifies: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs (DFS does not — incorrect, not slow); DFS is natural for cycle detection, topological sort, and post-order tree aggregation; space tradeoff depends on graph shape. Concrete examples required.

rubric.A: Identifies a scenario where wrong choice produces incorrect result (not just slower), describes 2+ correct-use cases for each, discusses space tradeoff
rubric.B: Describes when each is appropriate with examples but doesn't identify an incorrectness scenario
rubric.C: Describes one traversal well but not the other, or gives only vague 'usually BFS/DFS is better for X' answers

M05.title: Assessment: Binary Search, Heaps, and Monotonic Stacks

questions.question: The condition `while lo < hi` (rather than `while lo <= hi`) is used in the leftmost-True binary search template. What does `lo` equal at loop termination?

options.A: `lo` equals the last index checked before the loop exited
options.B: `lo == hi`, pointing to the leftmost position where the predicate is True
options.C: `lo == hi - 1`, pointing one before the answer
options.D: `lo` equals -1 if the target is not found

questions.explanation: With `while lo < hi`, when the loop exits, `lo == hi`. The loop maintains: everything left of `lo` is False and everything from `lo` onward may be True. At termination, `lo` is the leftmost True position (or n if no True exists).

questions.question: You need to binary search on the answer space for 'minimum integer k such that some_predicate(k) is True'. The predicate is monotone (once True, stays True). What do you set as `lo` and `hi`?

options.A: `lo = 0, hi = len(input) - 1` as in standard array binary search
options.B: `lo` = minimum possible answer value, `hi` = maximum possible answer value + 1
options.C: `lo = 1, hi = max(input)` regardless of the problem
options.D: Binary search on the answer space is not valid — enumerate all possible k values

questions.explanation: Binary search on answer space: lo = lower bound of valid answers (often 1 or 0), hi = upper bound + 1 (exclusive, e.g. max(piles)+1 in Koko problem). The monotone predicate ensures the standard leftmost-True template applies.

questions.question: You need the Kth largest element in a stream of integers. Which data structure maintains the answer in O(log K) per insertion?

options.A: Max-heap of all elements seen so far — root is always the largest
options.B: Min-heap of size K — root is the Kth largest
options.C: Sorted list — find Kth largest by index
options.D: Max-heap of size K — root is the Kth largest

questions.explanation: A min-heap of size K holds the K largest elements seen so far. The root is the smallest of those K elements — i.e. the Kth largest. For each new element: if larger than root, pop and push. O(log K) per insertion. Max-heap of size K would give the largest, not the Kth largest from root.

questions.question: Python's `heapq` module implements a min-heap. You need to use it as a max-heap. What is the standard workaround?

options.A: Use `heapq.heappush_max()` which exists as a built-in counterpart
options.B: Negate the values before pushing and negate again after popping
options.C: Reverse the list after `heapify` to convert min-heap to max-heap
options.D: Use `sorted()` with `reverse=True` instead of heapq for max-heap behaviour

questions.explanation: Python has no built-in max-heap. The standard pattern: push `-value` to make larger values sort first (min-heap treats -10 as smaller than -5, so -10 is at root → actual max 10 is at root). Negate again on pop to recover the original value.

questions.question: The monotonic stack for 'Next Greater Element' processes `[2, 1, 2, 4, 3]`. What is in the stack immediately after processing the element `4` (index 3)?
questions.explanation: Stack before index 3: [2, 1, 2] (indices 0,1,2). Process 4: pop while stack top < 4: pop 2 (NGE=4), pop 1 (NGE=4), pop 2 (NGE=4). Stack empty, push 4. Stack = [4]. 4 has no NGE yet since it hasn't been popped.

questions.question: Why is the total time complexity of the monotonic stack NGE algorithm O(n) even though there is a while loop inside the for loop?

options.A: The while loop only runs for the first and last element, so it runs O(1) total
options.B: Each element is pushed to the stack at most once and popped at most once — O(2n) = O(n) total work
options.C: The while loop runs at most log n times per element due to the monotone ordering
options.D: The stack never holds more than 2 elements at a time, bounding the while loop

questions.explanation: Amortised analysis: over all n iterations of the outer for loop, each element is pushed exactly once and popped at most once. Total push+pop operations = O(2n) = O(n), regardless of how many pops happen per iteration.

questions.question: Which problem property should trigger the thought 'I might need a monotonic stack'?

options.A: The problem involves finding maximum/minimum in a sliding window
options.B: For each element, find the nearest element to its left or right that is greater or smaller
options.C: The array must be processed in sorted order
options.D: The problem requires finding all pairs with a given sum

questions.explanation: The monotonic stack recognition pattern: 'nearest element to the left/right that satisfies some ordering property (greater/smaller/equal).' Examples: Daily Temperatures (next warmer), Largest Rectangle in Histogram (nearest shorter bar), Next Greater Element.

questions.question: Describe the three modification points in the unified binary search template (`lo`, `hi`, `return value`) and explain what to change for: (a) find the leftmost True, (b) find the rightmost True.

required_points: Three points: initial hi value, the case when predicate is True (set hi=mid or lo=mid+1), and what to return at the end
(a) Leftmost True: hi = n, when True: hi = mid, return lo
(b) Rightmost True: lo = -1, when True: lo = mid, when False: hi = mid - 1, return lo
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May describe in code form; exact variable names may differ
answer_key.full_credit: Correctly describes both variants with the right boundary movements
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly describes one variant

questions.question: Explain why building a heap with heapify is O(n) rather than O(n log n), and describe when you should use heapify instead of inserting elements one by one.

required_points: heapify sifts down from bottom up: most nodes are at lower levels with small subtrees → O(1) sift cost. Total = sum of sift costs = O(n) (series converges). Inserting n elements one by one: each insert is O(log n), total O(n log n).
Use heapify when you have all n elements upfront: O(n) vs O(n log n) for sequential inserts
Use sequential inserts for streaming data where elements arrive one at a time
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Geometric series convergence argument accepted without full proof
answer_key.full_credit: Correct complexity, correct intuition, correct decision rule for when to use each
answer_key.partial_credit: States O(n) without justification but correctly describes when to use heapify

questions.prompt: You are interviewing and are given a problem you haven't seen before. Describe how you would diagnose whether the correct tool is binary search, a heap, or a monotonic stack. What specific cues in the problem statement trigger each, and what is a common mistake when misidentifying the tool?
questions.guidance: Strong answer: binary search cues (sorted array, monotone predicate, answer space), heap cues (streaming top-K, priority queue, merge K sorted), monotonic stack cues (nearest greater/lesser, histogram, span). Common mistakes: using sorted() for each query instead of heap for running top-K; using nested loops instead of monotonic stack for NGE.

rubric.A: Gives recognition signals for all 3 tools with concrete examples, identifies at least one common misidentification mistake per tool
rubric.B: Gives recognition signals for all 3 tools with examples but doesn't identify mistakes
rubric.C: Accurately describes 2 of 3 tools or gives signals without concrete examples

M06.title: Assessment: Complexity Analysis — Big-O, Master Theorem, Amortised

questions.question: f(n) = 3n² + 100n + 500. Which of the following is the correct Big-O classification?

options.A: O(3n²) because the leading coefficient matters
options.B: O(n² + n) because lower-order terms affect growth for small n
options.C: O(n²) — dominant term only, constant factors and lower-order terms dropped
options.D: O(n³) — always round up to a higher order for safety

questions.explanation: Big-O drops constant factors and lower-order terms. For large n, 3n² dominates 100n and 500. Constants 3, 100, 500 are absorbed by the c in the formal definition. O(n²) is the correct tightest Big-O upper bound.

questions.question: A coding problem states n ≤ 10⁶. Your current solution is O(n log n). Will it pass within 1 second (assuming ~10⁸ simple operations/second)?

options.A: No — n log n with n=10⁶ ≈ 2×10⁷ operations, which exceeds 10⁸
options.B: Yes — n log n with n=10⁶ ≈ 2×10⁷ operations, well within 10⁸
options.C: Impossible to determine without knowing the constant factor
options.D: No — O(n log n) is too slow for n > 10⁴

questions.explanation: n=10⁶, log₂(10⁶) ≈ 20. n log n ≈ 2×10⁷ operations — well within the ~10⁸ budget. O(n²) = 10¹² would TLE. O(n log n) is the standard sorting-based upper limit that passes for n up to ~10⁷.

questions.question: Apply the Master Theorem to T(n) = 4T(n/2) + O(n). What is the tight bound?

options.A: Θ(n log n) — Case 2
options.B: Θ(n²) — Case 1 (recursive work dominates)
options.C: Θ(n log² n)
options.D: Θ(n) — Case 3 (f(n) dominates)

questions.explanation: a=4, b=2, log₂(4)=2. Compare f(n)=O(n)=O(n¹) to n^log_b(a) = n². Since n¹ = O(n^(2-ε)) for ε=1, Case 1 applies: T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a)) = Θ(n²). The recursive subproblems dominate.

questions.question: A binary search tree's height is O(log n) for a balanced tree but O(n) for a skewed tree. What does this imply about BST lookup complexity?

options.A: BST lookup is always O(log n) — the binary search property ensures this
options.B: BST lookup is O(h) where h is the height — O(log n) for balanced, O(n) worst-case for skewed
options.C: BST lookup is Θ(log n) average across all possible insertions
options.D: BST lookup is O(1) amortised for red-black trees regardless of height

questions.explanation: BST lookup traverses one root-to-node path of length h. Balanced BST (AVL, red-black): O(log n). Skewed insertion order (1,2,3,4,5 into a BST): degenerates to a linked list, h=n, O(n) lookup.

questions.question: Union-Find with both path compression and union by rank has O(α(n)) amortised complexity. In practice for all feasible n, α(n) ≤ 5. What does this mean for interview complexity discussions?

options.A: State that DSU is O(1) amortised since α(n) is so small it is negligible
options.B: State O(α(n)) precisely — interviewers know this notation and it signals rigour
options.C: Always state O(log n) as a conservative safe estimate
options.D: Avoid DSU in interviews since its complexity is too complex to explain

questions.explanation: Saying O(α(n)) is precise and expected for DSU. Saying O(1) is technically wrong (it's a soft approximation). O(log n) overstates the cost. Using the correct notation signals understanding of amortised analysis and the inverse Ackermann function.

questions.question: Which of the following best distinguishes Θ (Theta) from O (Big-O)?

options.A: O gives the exact running time; Θ gives the worst-case upper bound
options.B: O is an upper bound (at most); Θ is both upper and lower (exactly this growth rate, up to constant)
options.C: Θ adds constant factors that O drops; they differ only for small n
options.D: O and Θ are interchangeable; industry uses O colloquially to mean Θ

questions.explanation: O(g): f ≤ c·g (upper bound). Ω(g): f ≥ c·g (lower bound). Θ(g): f is both O(g) and Ω(g) — tight bound. In practice, interviewers say 'O(n log n)' but often mean Θ. Knowing the distinction — and being precise when asked — demonstrates theoretical rigour.

questions.question: The amortised cost of Python list.append() is O(1). A colleague argues: 'But occasionally it is O(n), so the amortised claim is misleading.' How do you respond?

options.A: You agree — O(1) amortised is just a mathematical shortcut that hides the real cost
options.B: You explain: amortised analysis accounts for the infrequent O(n) resizes. The per-operation average over n appends is O(1) — a worst-case guarantee on the average, not an approximation
options.C: You agree that append is O(n) worst-case and recommend pre-allocating lists
options.D: Amortised analysis is approximate; the colleague is correct to be skeptical

questions.explanation: Amortised O(1) is a rigorous guarantee: the total cost of n appends is O(n) (table doubling ensures each element is copied O(1) times on average). It's not approximate — infrequent expensive operations are paid for by the 'credit' saved during cheap operations.

questions.question: State the three cases of the Master Theorem for T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) and give a concrete example algorithm for each case.

required_points: Case 1: f(n) = O(n^(log_b(a) - ε)) → T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a)). Example: naive Strassen 4T(n/2)+O(n) = Θ(n²)
Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a) · log^k(n)) → T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a) · log^(k+1)(n)). Example: merge sort 2T(n/2)+O(n) = Θ(n log n)
Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b(a) + ε)) and regularity condition → T(n) = Θ(f(n)). Example: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(n) = Θ(n)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Exact wording of conditions may vary; any correct algorithm example for each case accepted
answer_key.full_credit: All three cases correctly stated with an example for each
answer_key.partial_credit: Two cases correctly stated with examples

questions.question: A function's inner loop runs O(log n) times for each of the n outer iterations: `for i in range(n): j=n; while j>1: j//=2`. State the total complexity and justify your answer with an explicit counting argument.

required_points: Inner while loop halves j each time → runs log₂(n) iterations
Outer for loop runs n times
Total: n × log₂(n) = O(n log n)
Justification: operations at each outer step = log₂(n); summing over all n steps = n·log n
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May write as sum ∑ₙ log n = n log n
answer_key.full_credit: Correct O(n log n) with explicit multiplication argument
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct answer without sufficient justification

questions.prompt: Why is amortised analysis more useful than worst-case analysis for data structures like dynamic arrays and Union-Find? Describe a scenario where relying on worst-case analysis alone would lead you to choose a worse data structure.
questions.guidance: Strong answer: worst-case for a single append is O(n) → suggests always using linked lists; amortised shows O(1) per append making arrays competitive. For DSU: log n per operation (no optimisations) vs O(α(n)) amortised with both optimisations — wrong data structure choice if comparing log n DSU to better alternatives.

rubric.A: Defines amortised vs worst-case clearly, gives concrete data structure example where worst-case misleads, identifies the correct data structure choice using amortised reasoning
rubric.B: Explains the concept correctly but gives only one example or the example is vague
rubric.C: Describes the difference between amortised and worst-case without applying it to a concrete data structure decision

M07.title: Assessment: Space Complexity and Recursion Depth

questions.question: A recursive DFS processes each node once in a balanced binary tree with n nodes. What is the space complexity from the call stack alone?

options.A: O(1) — DFS does not allocate additional memory
options.B: O(n) — all nodes can be on the call stack simultaneously
options.C: O(log n) — the call stack depth equals the tree height for a balanced tree
options.D: O(n log n) — each level of the tree contributes log n frames

questions.explanation: Call stack depth = maximum number of simultaneously active recursive calls = path from root to deepest node = tree height h. For a balanced binary tree, h = O(log n), so call stack space = O(log n). Only one root-to-leaf path is active at a time.

questions.question: The same DFS on a degenerate (linked-list shaped) binary tree with n nodes. What is the call stack space complexity?

options.A: O(log n) — tree balance guarantees O(log n) stack depth
options.B: O(1) — the iterative stack is O(n) but the recursive call stack is O(1)
options.C: O(n) — the only path from root to leaf has length n
options.D: O(n²) — each node processes its parent, doubling the stack

questions.explanation: Degenerate tree = each node has only one child. The single path from root to leaf has length n-1. The entire path is on the call stack simultaneously. Space = O(n). This is why you cannot always claim O(log n) recursive space for trees.

questions.question: Your Python DFS solution raises `RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded` on a test with n=10,000 nodes and a degenerate tree. What is the most robust fix?

options.A: Add `sys.setrecursionlimit(100000)` at the start of the script
options.B: Convert the DFS to iterative using an explicit stack list (heap-allocated)
options.C: Add `sys.setrecursionlimit(100000)` and also increase thread stack size
options.D: Use BFS instead — it automatically avoids recursion depth issues for all tree problems

questions.explanation: The OS imposes a hard limit on the C call stack (typically ~8MB, ~20K–40K Python frames). `sys.setrecursionlimit` raises Python's internal limit but not the OS limit. Converting to iterative with a `stack = []` list moves depth tracking to the heap (no OS limit). BFS changes the traversal order and may not be valid for all use cases.

questions.question: BFS on a wide, shallow tree (all n nodes at depth 1) uses how much queue space?

options.A: O(1) — BFS only holds one level at a time
options.B: O(n) — all n leaves are in the queue simultaneously at depth 1
options.C: O(log n) — BFS has the same space as the tree height
options.D: O(n²) — each level references all prior levels in the queue

questions.explanation: BFS space = maximum queue size = maximum width of the graph. For a 'star' tree with n-1 leaves: after processing the root, all n-1 leaves are enqueued simultaneously. Queue space = O(n). This is the worst case for BFS, and the best case for DFS (O(1) for this tree).

questions.question: For which graph shape is DFS more space-efficient than BFS?

options.A: A linear chain (path graph) with n nodes
options.B: A complete graph where every node connects to every other node
options.C: A balanced binary tree
options.D: A star graph with one hub and n-1 leaf nodes

questions.explanation: Balanced binary tree: DFS stack depth = O(log n) (tree height). BFS queue at bottom level = O(n/2) = O(n). DFS wins for balanced trees. For a linear chain: DFS = O(n) stack, BFS = O(1) queue (BFS wins). For a star: DFS = O(1), BFS = O(n) (DFS wins again — D would also work).

questions.question: A recursive function `f(n)` calls itself once with `f(n-1)` and holds O(1) local data per frame. If Python raises RecursionError at n=5000, what is the implied stack depth?

options.A: 5000 frames — each recursive call is one frame
options.B: 5001 frames — the initial call plus 5000 recursive calls
options.C: ~5000 live frames, since f(n) needs f(n-1) through f(0) all on the stack simultaneously
options.D: ~1000 frames — Python's default recursion limit is 1000

questions.explanation: f(5000) calls f(4999) which calls f(4998) ... down to f(0). All calls are live simultaneously: f(5000) hasn't returned yet when f(0) is executing. Stack depth = n+1 frames. Python's default limit is 1000; `sys.setrecursionlimit` can raise this and the error at n=5000 confirms the limit is somewhere around 5000.

questions.question: An algorithm visits every cell in an m×n grid using recursive DFS. What is the call stack space complexity?

options.A: O(m×n) worst case — the entire grid could be a single path
options.B: O(min(m,n)) — the stack depth is bounded by the shorter dimension
options.C: O(m+n) — stack depth is bounded by the perimeter
options.D: O(log(m×n)) — DFS on grids is inherently O(log n)

questions.explanation: DFS call stack depth = maximum depth of the DFS tree = maximum path length in the grid. In the worst case (infinite recursion allowed), a single DFS path visits all m×n cells (e.g. a spiral). Call stack space = O(m×n). This is why grid DFS Python solutions often hit recursion limits.

questions.question: You have a correct recursive DFS function. Show the general template for converting it to iterative DFS using an explicit stack, preserving the same traversal semantics.

required_points: Replace recursive call with an explicit stack: `stack = [start_node]`
Loop: `while stack: node = stack.pop()` (replaces the recursive frame)
Push children/neighbors: `for neighbor in graph[node]: if neighbor not in visited: stack.append(neighbor)`
Mark visited either before pushing or immediately after popping (to avoid duplicates)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Code or pseudocode both accepted; exact visited-marking position noted with tradeoff explanation if mentioned
answer_key.full_credit: Stack initialisation, pop, process, push children pattern all present
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct concept but missing visited check or wrong push order

questions.question: Explain the space complexity tradeoff between BFS and DFS for graph traversal, and state which you would choose for: (a) a graph known to be wide and shallow, (b) a graph known to be deep and narrow.

required_points: BFS space = O(max width w) of the graph; DFS space = O(max depth d)
(a) Wide and shallow: depth d is small → DFS uses O(d) space (better). BFS would hold the entire wide level in queue
(b) Deep and narrow: width w is small → BFS uses O(w) space (better). DFS call stack = O(d) = large
answer_key.acceptable_variations: O(B·d) vs O(b^d) notation from tree search is acceptable if correctly applied
answer_key.full_credit: Correctly states O(w) BFS and O(d) DFS, correctly identifies which to use for each shape
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct tradeoff stated without both correct application examples

questions.prompt: Most candidates state 'O(1) extra space' for recursive algorithms. Explain why this is almost always wrong and describe what should be said instead. When, if ever, is a recursive algorithm genuinely O(1) extra space (excluding the call stack)?
questions.guidance: Strong answer: recursive algorithms use the call stack — O(h) for tree DFS, O(n) for linear recursion. 'Extra space' should be qualified as 'O(1) auxiliary space excluding the call stack, O(h) total including the call stack.' Tail recursion optimisation (TCO) in langs like Scheme creates O(1) stack space — Python does not do TCO.

rubric.A: Explains the call stack issue clearly, gives the correct phrasing with qualification, identifies TCO as the one genuine O(1) exception and notes Python doesn't support it
rubric.B: Explains the call stack issue and gives correct phrasing but doesn't discuss TCO
rubric.C: Identifies the issue without a concrete corrected formulation

M08.title: Assessment: Dynamic Programming — The Three-Stage Pipeline

questions.question: You see an optimisation problem that asks for the maximum profit from buying and selling stock. Which two properties would confirm it is solvable with DP?

options.A: The array is sorted and has no duplicates
options.B: Optimal substructure: optimal solution uses optimal sub-decisions; overlapping subproblems: same sub-computations recur
options.C: The problem involves a graph with weighted edges
options.D: The answer is a single integer (not a sequence), indicating DP over enumeration

questions.explanation: DP requires both properties. Optimal substructure: maximum profit on days 1..i depends on optimal decisions on days 1..i-1. Overlapping subproblems: without caching, the naive recursion recomputes the same sub-results. A sorted array or integer answer does not by itself indicate DP.

questions.question: Adding `@functools.cache` to a correct recursive function converts it to memoised DP. What is the time complexity of the memoised Fibonacci using this approach?

options.A: O(2^n) — the same as the non-memoised version
options.B: O(n) — each Fibonacci number is computed exactly once and cached
options.C: O(n log n) — cache lookup adds log n overhead per call
options.D: O(1) — the cache stores all results after the first full run

questions.explanation: fib(n) without caching has O(2^n) calls (exponential branching). With caching, each unique argument n is computed exactly once. There are exactly n+1 unique arguments (0..n). Total time: O(n) calls × O(1) per call = O(n).

questions.question: You are implementing 0/1 Knapsack in 1D DP. The inner loop iterates `for cap in range(W, weight-1, -1)` in reverse. What happens if you iterate forward instead?

options.A: The result is the same — forward vs reverse makes no difference for 0/1 knapsack
options.B: Items can be used multiple times (unbounded knapsack behaviour) because earlier updates pollute later lookups in the same row
options.C: The algorithm produces an off-by-one error on the boundary condition
options.D: The algorithm runs faster due to better cache locality

questions.explanation: In 1D DP, dp[cap] = max(dp[cap], dp[cap-weight]+value). Forward iteration: when computing dp[cap], dp[cap-weight] may already reflect the current item being added this iteration → item counted twice (unbounded knapsack). Reverse ensures dp[cap-weight] is from the previous item's state.

questions.question: Coin Change problem: given coins=[1,3,4] and amount=6. Greedy (always take largest coin ≤ remaining) takes: 4+1+1=3 coins. What does DP find?

options.A: 3 coins — same as greedy, confirming greedy is optimal
options.B: 2 coins: 3+3=2 — DP finds the global optimum that greedy misses
options.C: 1 coin: 6=6 — DP finds a denomination that greedy doesn't see
options.D: 4 coins — greedy was better; DP is suboptimal for coin change

questions.explanation: DP recurrence: dp[0]=0, dp[v] = min(dp[v-c]+1) for each coin c. dp[6] via paths: dp[3]+1=2 (using coin 3 twice). Greedy takes 4 first, leaving 2, then 1+1. DP finds 3+3=2 coins. This is the canonical counterexample showing greedy fails on non-canonical coin sets.

questions.question: You need to optimise dp space for Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) from O(m×n) to O(min(m,n)). What property of the recurrence makes this possible?

options.A: LCS only depends on adjacent characters, so two variables suffice
options.B: LCS dp[i][j] depends only on dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], and dp[i][j-1] — only the current and previous row needed
options.C: LCS can be solved greedily in O(n) without DP
options.D: LCS values are monotonically increasing, allowing binary search over the table

questions.explanation: LCS recurrence: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1 if match, else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Only row i-1 is needed to compute row i. Keep two 1D arrays (or one with careful update order), reducing space from O(m×n) to O(n) (or O(min(m,n)) by iterating over the shorter string).

questions.question: For Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown, a 1D dp[i] representing max profit on day i is insufficient. What additional dimension is needed and why?

options.A: dp[i][price] — track the current stock price to make the sell decision
options.B: dp[i][state] where state ∈ {holding, sold, rest} — profit depends on which state you are in on that day
options.C: dp[i][transactions] — number of transactions alters what actions are available
options.D: dp[i][cash] — cash balance determines whether you can buy

questions.explanation: After selling (sold state), you must wait one day (rest state) before buying again (→ holding state). The available transitions differ per state: you can only buy when in rest, sell when holding, etc. 1D dp[i] can't distinguish these — you need dp[i][state].

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of top-down memoisation over bottom-up tabulation?

options.A: Top-down always uses less memory
options.B: Top-down only computes subproblems that are actually needed; some subproblems may never be reached
options.C: Top-down is always faster due to hash map caching vs array access
options.D: Top-down avoids the need to identify base cases

questions.explanation: Top-down is lazy: it only computes a subproblem when first accessed. If the optimal solution requires only a fraction of all subproblems, top-down avoids the rest. Bottom-up fills the entire table even if most entries are unused. This matters for sparse problems like game trees.

questions.question: Write the memoised top-down Fibonacci function in Python using @cache. Then state its time complexity, space complexity (including call stack), and the base cases.

required_points: from functools import cache
@cache decorator on the function
def fib(n): if n <= 1: return n; return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
Time: O(n) — each n computed once
Space: O(n) auxiliary for cache + O(n) call stack = O(n) total
Base cases: fib(0) = 0, fib(1) = 1
answer_key.acceptable_variations: @lru_cache(maxsize=None) also accepted; base cases may include fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1 or fib(0)=1, fib(1)=1 (Fibonacci variant noted)
answer_key.full_credit: Correct code with @cache, correct base cases, correct complexities for both time and space
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct code but missing complexity analysis or incorrect complexity

questions.question: Describe the three-stage DP problem-solving pipeline (recognition, model, implement) and explain what happens if you skip the middle stage and go directly from recognition to implementation.

required_points: Stage 1 Recognition: identify optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems
Stage 2 Model: define dp[...] precisely, write the recurrence, identify base cases, determine evaluation order
Stage 3 Implement: code the recurrence (either memoised or tabulated)

Skipping Stage 2: without a precise state definition and recurrence, code becomes trial-and-error. Common failure: wrong state dimension, missing cases, incorrect base case → wrong answer with difficult-to-debug logic
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Stage names may vary; emphasise the necessity of state definition before coding
answer_key.full_credit: All three stages described accurately, consequence of skipping Stage 2 explained
answer_key.partial_credit: Two stages correctly described

questions.prompt: A colleague always writes tabulation (bottom-up) DP and never uses memoisation, arguing it is always faster. Evaluate this claim. When is tabulation preferable, when is memoisation preferable, and what property of the problem determines the choice?
questions.guidance: Strong answer: tabulation iterative = no call stack, easier space optimisation (rolling array), cache-friendly array access. Memoisation = only compute needed subproblems, natural recursion structure matches problem. Key property: if all states must be computed (dense subproblem graph), tabulation wins. If many states never reached (sparse subproblem graph), memoisation wins.

rubric.A: Evaluates both approaches with specific advantages, identifies the dense/sparse subproblem property as the deciding factor with an example
rubric.B: Correctly describes when each is better but uses vague criteria instead of the density of subproblem access
rubric.C: Describes one approach as universally better without engaging the tradeoff

questions.question: What is the time complexity of `result = []` followed by `result.insert(0, x)` called n times?

options.A: O(n) total — insert is O(1) amortised like append
options.B: O(n²) total — each `insert(0, x)` shifts all existing elements right in O(n)
options.C: O(n log n) total — insert uses binary search internally
options.D: O(n) total if the list was pre-allocated with n slots

questions.explanation: `list.insert(0, x)` shifts every existing element one position right: O(k) for a list of k elements. Over n insertions: 0+1+2+...+(n-1) = O(n²). Fix: `result.append(x)` then `result.reverse()` — O(n) total.

questions.question: Python code: `result = ''; for s in string_list: result += s`. What is the time complexity and why?

options.A: O(n) — string += is equivalent to list.append, amortised O(1)
options.B: O(n²) — each += creates a new string object copying all previous characters, total characters copied = 1+2+...+n
options.C: O(n log n) — Python optimises string concatenation with a rope
options.D: O(n) in CPython 3.x due to the PEP 393 compact string optimisation

questions.explanation: Python strings are immutable. Each `result += s` allocates a new string object of length len(result)+len(s) and copies all characters. Total copies: sum of lengths = O(n²) in the worst case (equal-length strings). Fix: `''.join(string_list)` — O(n) total.

questions.question: In Java, `int lo = 1_500_000_000; int hi = 1_500_000_000; int mid = (lo + hi) / 2;`. What is the value of mid?

options.A: 1,500,000,000 — correct midpoint
options.B: -647,483,648 — integer overflow: lo+hi = 3,000,000,000 > Integer.MAX_VALUE (2,147,483,647)
options.C: 750,000,000 — Java silently promotes to long for arithmetic
options.D: A compile error — Java does not allow literals > 2,147,483,647

questions.explanation: Java int: MAX_VALUE = 2,147,483,647. lo+hi = 3,000,000,000 overflows to a negative value (wraps around). mid = negative/2 = negative. Fix: `int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;` — no overflow since hi-lo ≤ MAX_VALUE.

questions.question: JavaScript: `[10, 9, 2, 1, 100].sort()`. What does this return?

options.D: TypeError — sort() requires a comparator

questions.explanation: JavaScript's default sort converts elements to strings and sorts lexicographically. '10' < '100' < '2' < '9' (string comparison). Fix: `arr.sort((a, b) => a - b)` for ascending numeric sort. This is one of the most common interview bugs in JavaScript.

questions.question: Python: `from collections import Counter; c = Counter('aabbc'); c['x']`. What does this return?

options.A: KeyError: 'x'
options.B: 0 — Counter returns 0 for missing keys
options.C: None
options.D: An empty Counter object

questions.explanation: Counter is a subclass of dict but returns 0 (the default) for missing keys rather than raising KeyError. This makes it convenient for frequency comparisons without explicit membership checks: `if c[char] > 0:` won't crash for unseen characters.

questions.question: Your Python backtracking function builds strings by `path += char` at each recursion level and reconstructs the result. What is the hidden space problem?

options.A: No problem — string += in backtracking is the recommended pattern
options.B: Each recursive call creates a new string object of increasing length: O(depth²) total allocation across all live frames
options.C: String immutability causes the backtrack step to fail — path doesn't restore itself
options.D: Python deduplicates equal string objects, causing incorrect results when path values collide

questions.explanation: At depth d, path has d characters. Creating a new string at each depth: 1+2+...+n = O(n²) total allocations for a single root-to-leaf path. In backtracking with many branches, this multiplies. Fix: use a list, append/pop, and `''.join(path)` only at leaf nodes.

questions.question: Python dict preserves insertion order as of Python 3.7. Which of the following is still O(n) and NOT O(1)?

options.A: d[key] = value  (insertion)
options.B: del d[key]  (deletion by key)
options.C: list(d.keys())[0]  (get first inserted key)
options.D: key in d  (membership test)

questions.explanation: `list(d.keys())` materialises all keys into a new list — O(n). Then `[0]` is O(1). The overall operation is O(n). All of insert, delete, and `in` on a dict are O(1) average. To get the first key in O(1), use `next(iter(d))`.

questions.question: List three Python-specific O(n) operations that are commonly mistaken for O(1), and for each, give the O(1) or faster alternative.

required_points: 1. list.pop(0) or list.insert(0, x) → use collections.deque with popleft()/appendleft(); O(1)
2. x in list → use x in set or x in dict; O(1) average
3. ''.join inside a loop with += → collect in list, join once; O(n) total vs O(n²)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: list.copy() O(n) is also valid; sorted() being O(n log n) when you only need min/max (use min/max O(n)) is also acceptable
answer_key.full_credit: Three correct pairs of O(n) operation and O(1) alternative
answer_key.partial_credit: Two correct pairs

questions.question: Explain why integer overflow is a significant risk in Java and C but not in Python, and describe the two most common interview locations where Java overflow appears.

required_points: Java int = 32-bit signed, max ~2.1×10⁹. Operations exceeding this silently wrap. Python integers have arbitrary precision — no overflow.
Location 1: binary search midpoint: (lo+hi)/2 overflows when lo+hi > MAX_VALUE
Location 2: large multiplication: (long)a * b in 64-bit problems; forgetting to cast before multiply
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May mention C/C++ undefined behaviour for signed overflow
answer_key.full_credit: Explains Python's arbitrary precision, identifies both overflow locations with fixes
answer_key.partial_credit: Explains the difference without identifying the interview-specific locations

questions.prompt: Language-specific quirks cause real interview failures. Select two languages (from Python, Java, and JavaScript) and for each: identify the single most dangerous performance trap for algorithm problems and the single most dangerous correctness trap. Explain why each trap is hard to notice without specific knowledge.
questions.guidance: Strong answer identifies: Python — deque vs list (perf), mutable default arguments or shallow copy (correctness). Java — string concatenation in loops (perf), integer overflow (correctness). JS — O(n²) spreading/slicing (perf), default sort (correctness). 'Hard to notice' explanation required for full marks.

rubric.A: Two languages covered, one perf trap and one correctness trap each, explains why each is non-obvious
rubric.B: Two languages covered with correct traps but missing the 'hard to notice' explanation
rubric.C: Only one language covered well, or traps identified without concrete examples

M10.title: Assessment: Backtracking, Union-Find, and Remaining Interview Patterns

questions.question: Your backtracking solution for Subsets collects results: `result.append(path)`. After running, all sublists in result are empty. What is the bug?

options.A: You forgot to initialise result before the backtrack call
options.B: path is a mutable list; you stored a reference that was later emptied. Fix: `result.append(path[:])`
options.C: Backtracking with lists is not possible — use strings for path tracking
options.D: The append should be at the end of the function, not inside the recursive call

questions.explanation: When you `result.append(path)`, you store a reference to the same list object. As the backtracking continues, path is modified (elements removed). By the time backtracking completes, path is empty, and all stored references point to the same empty list. Fix: store a copy with `path[:]`.

questions.question: In a backtracking template: choose → explore → unchoose. What is the purpose of the 'unchoose' (undo) step?

options.A: To improve time complexity by reusing partial computations
options.B: To restore the state so that when the recursive call returns, the next sibling branch explores from the same precondition
options.C: To mark the current choice as invalid so it is not chosen again in future branches
options.D: To handle base cases where no valid choice exists

questions.explanation: Backtracking explores a search tree. After exploring all paths through a choice, undoing it restores the state for the next sibling choice. Without undo, each branch would see the effects of previous branches — paths would concatenate incorrectly.

questions.question: Union-Find `union(u, v)` returns False (u and v already in the same set). You are using this inside an algorithm that processes graph edges. What does a False return tell you?

options.A: u and v are in different components — this edge should be added to the spanning tree
options.B: u and v are in the same component — adding this edge creates a cycle
options.C: The union-find data structure is corrupted and should be re-initialised
options.D: The edge (u, v) is a self-loop and should be ignored

questions.explanation: DSU.union(u, v) returns False when find(u) == find(v) — both already share a root. If you add this edge to a graph, you create a cycle. This is used in Kruskal's MST (skip edges that would create cycles) and in Redundant Connection problems.

questions.question: Path compression in Union-Find modifies the structure during `find()`. What change does it make?

options.A: It sorts the parent array to enable binary search for root finding
options.B: It relabels roots with lower numbers to reduce rank
options.C: It relinks every node on the find path directly to the root, flattening the tree
options.D: It merges the two subtrees at the point of first common ancestor

questions.explanation: During `find(x)`, as you traverse the chain x → parent[x] → ... → root, path compression sets `parent[x] = root` (and all intermediate nodes) directly — flat tree for future queries. This amortises to O(α(n)) per operation combined with union by rank.

questions.question: For a general interview-prep sprint with limited time, where should dynamic programming usually sit in your study order?

options.A: Before hash maps, traversal, and binary search, because DP is the most transferable pattern
options.B: After the higher-frequency core patterns are solid, unless company-specific signals justify moving it earlier
options.C: Only after advanced graph algorithms and bit manipulation
options.D: It should be skipped entirely for general SWE interviews

questions.explanation: The evidence supports the DP learning pipeline strongly, but it also says DP is usually sequenced after higher-frequency interview patterns for short prep windows. Google-style or more advanced targets are the main reason to move it earlier.

questions.question: What is the best default linked-list technique when an insertion, deletion, or merge might change the head node?

options.A: Dummy-head or sentinel-node handling
options.B: Monotonic stack for next-greater-element problems
options.C: Union by rank in DSU
options.D: Prefix sums for subarray counting

questions.explanation: A dummy or sentinel node removes special-case handling at the true head. That makes insertion, deletion, and merge routines cleaner because every pointer attachment has the same shape.

questions.question: Where do the current study-sequence signals place tries?

options.A: Before trees, because tries are the simpler prerequisite
options.B: Alongside hash maps as a first-wave must-learn topic
options.C: After trees as a later, mid-priority follow-up topic
options.D: Outside the interview-prep sequence entirely

questions.explanation: NeetCode places tries after trees, and the other prioritisation material treats them as a later follow-up rather than a first-wave pattern. They matter, but they do not displace the highest-ROI core topics.

questions.question: Write the Union-Find class in Python with path compression and union by rank. Include find(), union(), and __init__() methods.

required_points: __init__(n): self.parent = list(range(n)); self.rank = [0]*n
find(x): path compression — while parent[x] != x: parent[x] = parent[parent[x]]; x = parent[x]; return x
union(x, y): rx, ry = find(x), find(y); if rx == ry: return False; if rank[rx] < rank[ry]: swap; parent[ry] = rx; if rank[rx] == rank[ry]: rank[rx]++; return True
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Recursive path compression (parent[x] = find(parent[x])) is also valid; exact variable names may differ
answer_key.full_credit: All three methods correct with both path compression and union by rank
answer_key.partial_credit: Two of three methods correct, or all three missing one optimisation

questions.question: State the two structural conditions that usually justify a greedy solution, and explain in 2–3 sentences what an exchange argument is.

required_points: Greedy-choice property
Optimal substructure
Exchange argument: start from an optimal solution, find the first place it differs from the greedy choice, swap in the greedy choice without making the solution worse, then continue inductively
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Equivalent wording is fine if it clearly names both structural conditions and explains the swap-based proof idea
answer_key.full_credit: Names both conditions and explains exchange argument as a proof that the greedy choice can replace the corresponding choice in an optimal solution without reducing quality
answer_key.partial_credit: Names one condition correctly or gives a partially correct description of the exchange proof idea

questions.prompt: You have finished the rebuilt course and want one focused second-wave study block. Rank linked lists, tries, and greedy for your next sprint, assuming general FAANG-style prep rather than a specialised competitive-programming track. Explain the ranking using frequency, study-sequence evidence, and the type of skill each topic develops.
questions.guidance: Strong answers recognise linked lists as the highest-priority of the three because they recur more often and stress pointer execution under pressure, with tries and greedy treated as real but lower-priority follow-up categories. Strong answers distinguish ranking by interview ROI from whether a topic is intellectually important.

rubric.A: Presents a defensible ranking, uses the roadmap and priority evidence, and distinguishes topic importance from study order
rubric.B: Ranking is plausible but the explanation does not clearly connect to the research signals
rubric.C: Ranking is unsupported, or ignores the sequencing evidence and the distinct skill each topic trains

M01.title: Module Check: The Interview Algorithm Landscape

questions.question: Name the 6 steps of the UMPIRE framework in order and state what happens at each step.
questions.answer: Understand (clarify the problem and constraints), Match (identify the pattern), Plan (outline the algorithm in pseudocode), Implement (write code), Review (trace through examples), Evaluate (state time/space complexity).

questions.question: What property must an input have for binary search to be applicable, and why does that property matter?
questions.answer: The input must be sorted (or have a monotonic property). This allows each comparison to eliminate half the remaining search space, achieving O(log n) instead of O(n).

questions.question: At which UMPIRE step should you write the first line of code, and what three steps must precede it?
questions.answer: I (Implement) — only after completing U (Understand), M (Match), and P (Plan). Writing code before planning leads to wasted effort on the wrong algorithm.

questions.question: According to the Pareto principle applied to algorithm interviews, approximately what share of problems can be solved with the top 20% of patterns?

options.A: ~50% of problems
options.B: ~65% of problems
options.C: ~80% of problems
options.D: ~95% of problems

questions.explanation: The Pareto principle (80/20 rule) holds here: mastering hash maps, two pointers, BFS/DFS, binary search, and DP covers the large majority of interview problems. 50% and 65% understate the leverage; 95% overstates it.

questions.question: During the Review step of UMPIRE, what is the primary goal?

options.A: Refactor the code to use fewer lines
options.B: Trace through the example inputs to verify correctness before the interviewer asks
options.C: Look up standard library functions you may have missed
options.D: Restate the problem to confirm you understood it

questions.explanation: Review means manually tracing your implementation against the given examples to catch off-by-one errors or missed edge cases. Refactoring and library lookup are secondary concerns; problem restatement belongs in the Understand step.

M02.title: Module Check: Hash Maps — The Universal Speed-Up

questions.question: Explain the complement lookup pattern. What makes it work, and which problem archetype does it solve?
questions.answer: For each element x, check whether target - x already exists in the map. It works because a single O(1) hash lookup replaces an inner O(n) scan. It solves pair-sum problems: Two Sum, variants with k difference, etc.

questions.question: When would you choose Counter over defaultdict(int) for frequency counting in Python?
questions.answer: Counter when you need built-in operations like most_common(), arithmetic between counters, or subtraction of counts. defaultdict(int) when you need a plain mutable mapping with no extra overhead and won't use Counter's extended API.

questions.question: What causes a hash map collision, and how do Python's dicts handle it?
questions.answer: A collision occurs when two keys hash to the same bucket index. Python dicts use open addressing with pseudo-random probing — the conflicting key is stored in another slot determined by the probe sequence, not a linked list.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of `x in some_list` versus `x in some_set` in Python?

options.A: O(1) for both
options.B: O(log n) for list, O(1) for set
options.C: O(n) for list, O(1) average for set
options.D: O(n) for both

questions.explanation: List membership requires a linear scan (O(n)); set membership uses a hash lookup (O(1) average). This is the core reason to convert a lookup list to a set before a repeated membership test.

questions.question: Which Python construct automatically initialises a missing key with an empty list?

options.A: collections.OrderedDict()
options.B: collections.defaultdict(list)
options.C: dict.setdefault(key, [])
options.D: dict.get(key, [])

questions.explanation: defaultdict(list) creates an empty list for any new key on first access. setdefault also works but requires explicit call each time. dict.get never stores the default. OrderedDict preserves insertion order but has no default-factory.

questions.question: What precondition must hold for converging two pointers (lo/hi) to solve a pair-sum problem, and why is that precondition necessary?
questions.answer: The array must be sorted. Sortedness lets you infer direction: if arr[lo]+arr[hi] < target, moving lo right increases the sum; if > target, moving hi left decreases it. Without sorted order, there's no valid inference and you'd need O(n²) brute force.

questions.question: State the prefix sum range query formula and explain why prefix[0] must be 0.
questions.answer: sum(l, r) = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]. prefix[0] = 0 represents the empty prefix (sum of zero elements), which makes range queries starting at index 0 consistent: sum(0, r) = prefix[r+1] - prefix[0] = prefix[r+1].

questions.question: In a variable sliding window, what is the general trigger for shrinking the left pointer, and what invariant does shrinking maintain?
questions.answer: Shrink when the window violates the constraint (e.g. sum too large, too many distinct chars). Shrinking restores the invariant: the window always represents a valid state. The inner while loop shrinks until valid, then the answer is recorded at the current right boundary.

questions.question: What is the time and space complexity of the fixed-size sliding window maximum sum algorithm?

options.A: O(n²) time, O(k) space
options.B: O(n log n) time, O(1) space
options.C: O(n) time, O(k) space
options.D: O(n) time, O(1) space

questions.explanation: A single pass (O(n)) adds the incoming element and subtracts the outgoing element to maintain the window sum — no extra storage needed beyond a few variables (O(1)).

questions.question: Which pattern is the correct recognition signal for using a prefix sum?

options.A: Find the maximum element in a subarray
options.B: Answer multiple range sum queries on a static array
options.C: Find a subarray with distinct elements
options.D: Detect a cycle in a linked list

questions.explanation: Prefix sums give O(1) per range sum query after O(n) preprocessing — ideal for multiple queries on an unchanging array. Max element needs a segment tree; distinct elements uses a sliding window; cycle detection uses Floyd's algorithm.

M04.title: Module Check: Tree and Graph Traversal — BFS/DFS Duality

questions.question: In DFS three-colour marking, what do white, gray, and black mean, and how does encountering a gray node prove a cycle?
questions.answer: White = unvisited, gray = on the current call stack, black = fully processed. A gray node means we reached an ancestor still in the current DFS path — a back edge — which proves a directed cycle.

questions.question: Why does BFS guarantee shortest path in an unweighted graph, and why does DFS not?
questions.answer: BFS explores all nodes at distance d before any at d+1, so the first time it reaches a node is via the minimum number of edges. DFS may reach a node via a longer path first, then never revisit it.

questions.question: After running Kahn's algorithm, len(result) < n. What does this reveal and why?
questions.answer: The graph contains a directed cycle. Nodes in a cycle never have their in-degree reach zero, so they are never enqueued. If result is shorter than the node count, the missing nodes are part of a cycle.

questions.question: Which traversal order processes all children before their parent node?

options.A: Pre-order DFS
options.B: In-order DFS
options.C: Post-order DFS
options.D: BFS level-order

questions.explanation: Post-order visits left subtree → right subtree → root. This means children are fully processed before the parent is visited — required for problems like tree height, diameter, and serialisation.

questions.question: In an adjacency list representation, what is the time complexity of iterating all neighbours of every node across the full graph?

options.A: O(V)
options.B: O(E)
options.C: O(V + E)
options.D: O(V × E)

questions.explanation: Visiting each node is O(V); iterating all edges (once per endpoint) is O(E). Together O(V+E) is the standard BFS/DFS complexity on an adjacency list. O(V²) would apply to an adjacency matrix.

M05.title: Module Check: Binary Search, Heaps, and Monotonic Stacks

questions.question: In the unified binary search template `while lo < hi`, what value does `lo` hold when the loop exits, and why is this useful?
questions.answer: lo == hi, pointing to the single remaining candidate — the answer position. This is useful because you don't need a separate post-loop check for a found/not-found case; the loop terminates with lo as the target index regardless of whether you're doing exact search or finding a boundary.

questions.question: To find the K largest elements in a stream, do you use a min-heap or max-heap of size K? Explain the reasoning.
questions.answer: A min-heap of size K. The root is always the Kth largest seen so far. When a new element arrives, compare it to the root: if larger, pop the root and push the new element. This maintains the K largest with O(log K) per insertion. A max-heap of size K would require you to pop K times to get the answer.

questions.question: Why is heapify O(n) rather than O(n log n), even though each sift-down is O(log n)?
questions.answer: In bottom-up heapify, most nodes are near the leaves and have small subtrees. The sift-down cost per node is proportional to its subtree height, and half the nodes are leaves (height 0). The sum converges as a geometric series: n/2·0 + n/4·1 + n/8·2 + … = O(n).

questions.question: What is the recognition signal for a monotonic stack problem?

options.A: The problem asks for a subarray with maximum sum
options.B: For each element, find the nearest element to the left or right that is greater or smaller
options.C: The problem involves merging overlapping intervals
options.D: The problem requires finding the median of a stream

questions.explanation: Monotonic stacks solve 'next greater/smaller element' queries in O(n) by maintaining a stack where elements are always in monotone order. Max subarray sum uses Kadane's; intervals use sorting; stream median uses two heaps.

questions.question: Which midpoint formula prevents integer overflow in binary search?

options.B: `lo + (hi - lo) // 2`
options.C: `hi - (hi - lo) // 2`

questions.explanation: `(lo + hi)` overflows when both values are near INT_MAX. `lo + (hi - lo) // 2` computes the offset first, keeping all values within range. Option C is equivalent but less idiomatic. Option A and D both suffer the same overflow risk.

questions.question: What is the formal definition of f(n) = O(g(n))?
questions.answer: There exist constants c > 0 and n0 >= 1 such that for all n >= n0, f(n) <= c*g(n). This means g(n) is an upper bound on f(n) beyond some threshold.

questions.question: What is amortised analysis and how does it differ from worst-case analysis?
questions.answer: Amortised measures the average cost per operation over a sequence of n operations -- a guarantee, not a probability. Worst-case measures a single operation worst possible cost. Dynamic array append is O(n) worst-case but O(1) amortised because resizes are rare.

questions.question: Apply the Master Theorem to T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n). Show which case applies and state the result.
questions.answer: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n. n^log_b(a) = n^1 = n. Since f(n) = Theta(n^log_b(a)), Case 2 applies, giving T(n) = Theta(n log n).

questions.question: If n <= 10^5, which is the slowest algorithm complexity that still passes within 1 second?

options.B: O(n log n)
options.C: O(n*sqrt(n))

questions.explanation: O(n log n) produces ~1.7x10^6 operations for n=10^5, well within the ~10^8 budget. O(n^2) gives 10^10 -- TLE.

questions.question: Which statement about Theta notation is correct?

options.A: f(n)=Theta(g(n)) means f is only an upper bound of g
options.B: f(n)=Theta(g(n)) means f grows both no faster and no slower than g
options.C: f(n)=Theta(g(n)) implies f(n)=g(n) exactly
options.D: Theta notation is equivalent to O notation

questions.explanation: Theta is a tight bound: f=Theta(g) means f=O(g) AND f=Omega(g). It characterises the exact growth rate.

M07.title: Module Check: Space Complexity and Recursion Depth

questions.question: What determines the call stack depth of a recursive DFS on a tree, and what are the best- and worst-case values?
questions.answer: The height h of the tree. Best case: O(log n) for a balanced tree. Worst case: O(n) for a degenerate (linear chain) tree.

questions.question: Why is `sys.setrecursionlimit(10**6)` insufficient for very deep Python recursion?
questions.answer: Python's limit controls the internal counter but the OS limits the C call stack (typically ~8MB, ~20K–40K Python frames). A very deep recursion hits the OS stack limit before Python's, causing a segfault rather than a clean RecursionError.

questions.question: What is the correct fix for a Python RecursionError on a large DFS?
questions.answer: Convert to iterative DFS using an explicit stack list (heap-allocated, not limited by the OS call stack). Each frame's local state (node, children index) goes on the explicit stack instead of the call stack.

questions.question: For a balanced binary tree with n nodes, compare BFS and DFS space complexity.

options.A: BFS: O(log n), DFS: O(n)
options.B: BFS: O(n), DFS: O(log n)
options.C: Both O(log n)
options.D: Both O(n)

questions.explanation: BFS holds the entire widest level in its queue — O(n/2) = O(n) for a balanced tree. DFS only holds one path from root to leaf on the call stack — O(log n) for balanced. DFS is more space-efficient on balanced trees.

questions.question: When is BFS preferred over DFS despite its higher space cost?

options.A: When you need to detect cycles
options.B: When you need the shortest path in an unweighted graph
options.C: When the graph has very high branching factor
options.D: When you need to perform topological sort

questions.explanation: BFS explores by level, so the first path found to any node is guaranteed the shortest (fewest edges). DFS may find a longer path first and never revisit. Cycle detection and topological sort both typically use DFS.

M08.title: Module Check: Dynamic Programming — The Three-Stage Pipeline

questions.question: What are the two conditions required for dynamic programming to apply?
questions.answer: Optimal substructure (the optimal solution to the full problem can be built from optimal solutions to subproblems) and overlapping subproblems (the same subproblems are solved multiple times in naive recursion).

questions.question: In 0/1 Knapsack 1D DP, why must the capacity loop iterate in reverse order?
questions.answer: Forward iteration would use the already-updated dp[w - weight] value from the current item, effectively allowing the item to be chosen multiple times. Iterating backwards ensures dp[w - weight] still reflects the previous row (item not included yet), enforcing the 0/1 constraint.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of top-down memoisation over bottom-up tabulation?
questions.answer: Top-down only computes subproblems that are actually needed (lazy evaluation). For problems with sparse subproblem graphs, this can save significant time. Bottom-up always fills all states, which is wasteful if most states are unreachable.

questions.question: Which Python decorator converts a recursive function to memoised DP in one line (Python 3.9+)?

options.A: @functools.lru_cache()
options.B: @functools.cache
options.C: @memoize
options.D: @cache(maxsize=None)

questions.explanation: `@functools.cache` is the simpler alias added in Python 3.9 — equivalent to `@lru_cache(maxsize=None)`. `@functools.lru_cache()` works in 3.8+ but requires call parentheses. `@memoize` and `@cache(maxsize=None)` are not stdlib.

questions.question: Which problem requires a 2D DP state because a 1D state is insufficient?

options.A: Fibonacci sequence
options.B: Climbing stairs (1 or 2 steps)
options.C: Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown
options.D: Maximum subarray (Kadane's)

questions.explanation: Stock with Cooldown needs state = (day, holding_status) — two independent dimensions. Fibonacci and Climbing Stairs only need the current index. Kadane's uses a single running max with no subproblem overlap.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of `list.pop(0)` and what is the O(1) alternative?
questions.answer: O(n) — shifts all remaining elements left. Use `collections.deque.popleft()` for O(1) amortised removal from the front.

questions.question: Why is string concatenation with `+=` in a loop O(n²) in Python, and what is the fix?
questions.answer: Strings are immutable; each `+=` allocates a new string and copies all characters so far. Total copies = 1+2+…+n = O(n²). Fix: collect parts in a list and call `''.join(parts)` at the end — O(n) total.

questions.question: What bug does `[10, 9, 2, 1, 100].sort()` introduce in JavaScript, and how do you fix it?
questions.answer: JS's default sort converts elements to strings and sorts lexicographically, giving [1, 10, 100, 2, 9]. Fix: `.sort((a, b) => a - b)` for ascending numeric sort.

questions.question: In Java binary search, what risk does `(lo + hi) / 2` carry?

options.A: Floating-point rounding error
options.B: Integer overflow producing a negative midpoint
options.C: Division truncates toward negative infinity
options.D: No risk — Java promotes to long automatically

questions.explanation: When lo and hi are both near Integer.MAX_VALUE (~2×10⁹), their sum overflows to a negative number. `lo + (hi - lo) / 2` avoids the overflow. Java does not auto-promote int arithmetic to long.

questions.question: Which Python operation is O(n) and commonly triggers accidental quadratic behaviour inside a loop?

options.A: `dict[key]` lookup
options.B: `set.add(elem)`
options.C: `list.insert(0, elem)`
options.D: `tuple[i]` index access

questions.explanation: `list.insert(0, elem)` shifts all existing elements one position right — O(n). Inside a loop it becomes O(n²). Using `collections.deque.appendleft()` is O(1). Dict lookup, set.add, and tuple indexing are all O(1) average.

M10.title: Module Check: Backtracking, Union-Find, and Remaining Interview Patterns

questions.question: In backtracking, why must you `result.append(path[:])` instead of `result.append(path)`?
questions.answer: path is a mutable list; appending the reference stores a pointer that gets modified (and eventually emptied) by later exploration. `path[:]` makes a shallow copy — a snapshot of the current state — that remains unchanged as recursion continues.

questions.question: Name the two optimisations that give Union-Find O(α(n)) amortised complexity and briefly describe each.
questions.answer: Path compression flattens the parent chain during find by repointing nodes toward the root.
